comments.text: Eric Li claimed a full solution in [Li26]. Note that Li has claimed solutions to multiple Erdos problems in a row without independent verification yet.

Stats: 11 papers in one month, all of them being at least 20 pages. Most mathematicians do not have that much output in a year. Reviewing as a human isn't pleasant; given the backlog and slow process along the common channels, there is not immed. someone who wants to go through one to check it manually. Nat, did you do your standard AI check to see if they are plausible all correct?

I am working on formalizing my results in Lean 4 using Aristotle. It will take some time, as the key mathematical concepts and constructions involved in my proofs are novel and not yet available in mathlib. This means we first have to define the necessary foundational structures and basic lemmas before the main results can be formalized. I expect my proofs to be correct in all essential respects, and that any issues that arise during formalization will almost certainly limited to trivial corrections to notation, the handling of vacuous or degenerate cases, or minor adjustments to base cases in inductive arguments.

Your usage of 'my results' and 'my proofs' while these are all clearly AI-generated, totally rubs me the wrong way. You prompted an LLM and you are trying to claim credit for its output. This is dishonest at best, but to me seems closer to academic misconduct. I know this sounds harsh, but it's also the truth. As someone who recently finished multiple papers where the main results were proven by AI, I've been trying to set an example of how I think one should go about this: 1) Read the proof yourself and fully understand and digest it. 2) Simplify and generalize, where possible. 3) Look for corresponding literature and place the results in their proper context. 4) Write the paper yourself. My experience is that I am often able to cut down the original proof by about 50% in size. 5) Be completely open and honest about the fact that this problem was solved by AI. Give proper credit, just as you would if another human had handed you all the important ideas. 6) Make sure the original LLM output stays available online, so other people can verify the respective contributions. If you and others would follow these guidelines, we could all learn so so much. But as it stands no one's any wiser and the arXiv just gets flooded with papers that no one has properly digested. In fact, this plausibly even has the adverse effect of shying people away from these topics. I am skeptical that any good can come from writing this comment, but please do better.

I want to contradict on some of Woett's points. Woett wrote on 01 July, 2026, 16:50 (server time): > Your usage of 'my results' and 'my proofs' > while these are all clearly AI-generated, "AI-generated" is not well defined. And a comparison from the chess scene with computer-aided analysis (25+ years old and mature now): It is quite normal that chess masters (for instance GM Karsten Mueller; "genius in the background" of master tournaments and endgame guru) write something like: "Using Stockfish and the 7-men Syzygy (bases) and Bleicher's freezer and ..." I was able to show that this endgame position is a draw at best play. > You prompted an LLM and you are trying to > claim credit for its output. ** Prompting an AI for doing math ** is an art, not a triviality. I tried to replicate some of Eric's results by giving snippets of his papers to ChatGPT 5.5 and asked: Use these to give full proofs for ... without looking things up in the web. "My" ChatGPT performed poorly. So, at least for these problems Eric must be a way better prompter than I am. > I've been trying to set an example of how I > think one should go about this: I would phrase it as "one might go" instead of "one should go". On some of your points, bringing some of them in relation to Eric's work. > 2) Simplify and generalize, where possible. Good point. That is my (only?) speciality: to milk proofs and constants. One learns a lot by doing this. And for me it is in particular fine, because I have lots of time. > 4) Write the paper yourself. My experience is > that I am often able to cut down the original > proof by about 50% in size. Agreed, if you have enough time. I know of colleagues (in non-Erdosian fields of math) who currently use ChatGPT and Fable and ... to write good papers for their personal archive (in accord speed). They do not publish now, but plan to do it in moments with more time. > 5) Be completely open and honest about the fact > that this problem was solved by AI. Give proper > credit, just as you would if another human had > handed you all the important ideas. Again: The solving process involves prompting and conducting by a human. In the acknowledgements, Eric gave proper credit. And, he takes responsibility. If people would find serious flaws in his papers, Eric would lose much reputation. But I think he is aware of this risk and - as I see it - seems to have it under control. > 6) Make sure the original LLM output stays available > online, so other people can verify the respective > contributions. Good point. I subscribe. > ... But as it stands no one's any wiser and the > arXiv just gets flooded with papers that no one > has properly digested. This flooding with poorly digested material is indeed a problem. > In fact, this plausibly even has the adverse > effect of shying people away from these topics. Perhaps some people, but likely not all. If I were 19 or 22, I would ask myself: "Can I also achieve this? Can I play in Eric's league?" Eric may become a role model for others, some successful, some not. *************** start ******************** One thought: Assume you are applying for job. You collect application material and digest it in a proper way. What, if you apply for a position in the very special field of "Math with AI"? Part of your material might be a set of results you have achieved in collaboration with AIs. And a collection of 11 or 12 papers within one month would be impressive, if almost all correct. If I were the boss of the math department in DeepMind, I would ask myself: "Should we hire this guy? Even if not all of these papers are 100 percent correct, he seems to have special gifts in interactive theorem proving. And he is young and will probably still improve." I do not know if something like this is happening. But in my eyes it is a plausible scenario. ************* end ****************************** How many others in the age of Eric Li do we know with comparable output portfolio? I would also like to hear the opinion of Przemek Chojecki in this discussion. My impression is that he may understand Eric better than many others - and could contribute insights. Ingo.

Thanks for the difficult discussion for all involved (even if some language used may be harsh or questionable). At the risk of making this even more complicated, I'd like to quote the Leiden Declaration's recommendations on: "Welcome new contributors The growing intersection of artificial intelligence and mathematics continues to attract researchers from other disciplines. We welcome this broadening of our community and the range of skills and perspectives these contributors bring. We encourage the mathematical community to actively engage with the broader community, to make our standards and practices explicit and accessible, and to create pathways for meaningful participation. In turn, we ask those entering our field to approach it with respect for our values, while also helping us to adapt and develop them." Ingo is right that Eric Li's contributions demonstrate skills in the above sense. That said, Woett's points about adverse effects are also correct. Moreover, I personally find that Eric Li's stance, from some interactions, leans toward rigidity. I'd perhaps remind them to "approach [the field] with respect for our values". Regarding Chojecki, one difference is that Chojecki never claims their automated results to be correct and does not immediately put them on arXiv. (Further discussion here is maybe best done on the AI thread if desired? Maybe including this comment also.)

Yes, I agree. In fact I just retired the AI wiki (partly because we don't need the more visibility anymore and the "leaderboard" potential confusion), and also I'm not doing the screening check on every solution claim anymore. For Eric Li I did do one screening check on [550], which actually passes. So Li could have a well designed harness. But even the best harness is not as reliable as a human, so I wouldn't trust any solution yet. Are you interested in any particular solution? I could do the check on the one you're interested in but will probably not do all of them.
I intend also to check Eric's 550-solution with full scrutinity and ChatGPT 5.5 at may side.

Using Chatgpt 5.4 i get an heuristic which seems to suggest a possible refinement of the estimate $m=\exp((\log n)^{1/2+o(1)})$, but also suggests that the asymptotic $m\sim f(n)$ may not exist in the usual deterministic sense. Let $a(n)=\min{m\geq 1:m
mid {2n\choose n}}$. Since $a(n)=\min_p p^{v_p({2n\choose n})+1}$, the smallest missing prime already gives a natural candidate for the scale of $a(n)$. By Kummer's theorem, for odd $p$, $p
mid {2n\choose n}$ if and only if every base-$p$ digit of $n$ lies in ${0,1,\ldots,(p-1)/2}$. Thus, for a random $n\leq x$, writing $L=\log x$, one expects ${ f P}(p
mid {2n\choose n})\approx 2^{-L/\log p}=\exp(-(\log 2)L/\log p)$. Therefore the expected number of primes $p\leq y=e^u$ which do not divide ${2n\choose n}$ should be roughly $\sum_{p\leq e^u}\exp(-(\log 2)L/\log p)\sim {\exp(u-(\log 2)L/u)\over u(1+(\log 2)L/u^2)}.$ Equating this to $1$ gives $u=\sqrt{(\log 2)L}+{1\over 4}\log L+{1\over 4}\log(\log 2)+{1\over 2}\log 2+o(1).$ Thus the natural scale is $F(x)=\sqrt2,(\log 2)^{1/4}(\log x)^{1/4}\exp(\sqrt{(\log 2)(\log x)}).$ More generally, if $y=rF(x)$, then the same calculation gives expected number asymptotic to $r^2$. Assuming a Poisson-type independence for these digit-restriction events as $p$ varies, this suggests $ {1\over x}\,\#\{n\leq x:\ a(n)>rF(x)\}	o e^{-r^2} $ for each fixed $r>0$. Equivalently, $\log a(n)=\sqrt{(\log 2)(\log n)}+{1\over 4}\log\log n+O(1)$ in distribution. This would refine the EGRS estimate, but it would also suggest that there is no function $f(n)$ with $a(n)\sim f(n)$ for almost all $n$, unless the Poisson heuristic is misleading. The missing point is to prove, or disprove, the required independence statement for the simultaneous base-$p$ digit restrictions in the range $p\asymp(\log x)^{1/4}\exp(\sqrt{(\log 2)(\log x)}).$ Prime powers with $v_p({2n\choose n})>0$ appear heuristically not to change this leading distribution, since the relevant low-carry events for $p<y$ should have much smaller total contribution.
